---
title: "Team"
---

```{=html}
<div style="padding: 5rem 0; background: white;">
  <div class="container">
    <div class="section-title-wrapper">
      <span class="section-badge badge-teal">Our People</span>
      <h2>Meet the Team</h2>
      <div class="gold-line"></div>
      <p style="color: #666; max-width: 700px; margin: 0 auto; font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1.8;">
        Our team brings together leading researchers, educators, and students
        dedicated to advancing the frontiers of risk analytics and actuarial
        science.
      </p>
    </div>

    <!-- ──── Prof. Rendani Mbuvha ──── -->
    <div class="row g-4 mb-4">
      <div class="col-lg-4">
        <div class="team-card">
          <div class="team-card-top"></div>
          <div style="padding: 2rem 1.5rem;">
            <img src="images/team/Rendani_M.jpg" alt="Prof. Rendani Mbuvha" class="team-avatar" style="object-fit: cover;">
            <div class="team-name">Prof. Rendani Mbuvha</div>
            <div class="team-role">Lab Director & Professor</div>
            <div class="team-affiliation">University of the Witwatersrand</div>
            <div>
              <span class="team-badge">Bayesian Methods</span>
<span class="team-badge">Machine Learning</span>
<span class="team-badge">Climate Risk</span>
<span class="team-badge">Actuarial Science</span>
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>
      </div>
      <div class="col-lg-8">
        <div class="team-bio" style="border: 1px solid #eee; border-radius: 12px; padding: 2rem; height: 100%; display: flex; flex-direction: column; justify-content: center;">
          <p style="color: #444; font-size: 0.95rem; line-height: 1.8; margin-bottom: 1rem;">
  Prof. Rendani Mbuvha serves as Lab Director and brings extensive cross-sector 
  experience spanning academia, industry, and international research institutions. 
  He is a qualified actuary,holding fellowships from both the Institute and Faculty 
  of Actuaries (UK) and the Actuarial Society of South Africa (FASSA) since 2015, 
  along with the Chartered Enterprise Risk Actuary designation. His academic 
  credentials include a BSc in Actuarial Science and Statistics from the 
  University of Cape Town (2008–2011), an MSc in Machine Learning from KTH Royal 
  Institute of Technology in Stockholm (2015–2017), and a PhD in Artificial 
  Intelligence from the University of Johannesburg (2018–2021), where he was 
  supported by a 2019 Google Africa PhD Fellowship.
</p>
<p style="color: #444; font-size: 0.95rem; line-height: 1.8; margin-bottom: 1rem;">
  He joined WITS as a lecturer in 2017, progressing to Associate Professor by 2022. 
  Simultaneously, he has held positions as Google DeepMind Academic Fellow at 
  Queen Mary University of London (2022–present), Research Fellow in AI and Applied 
  Statistics at the United Nations University Institute for Water, Environment and 
  Health in Canada (2024–present), and since January 2025, Associate Professor in 
  Actuarial Science at the University of Manchester. He is also co-founder of 
  AfriClimate AI, a grassroots community advancing AI applications for climate 
  resilience across Africa.
</p>
<p style="color: #444; font-size: 0.95rem; line-height: 1.8;">
  His research output is substantial,over 30 peer-reviewed publications spanning 
  seasonal forecasting, Bayesian neural networks, Hamiltonian Monte Carlo methods, 
  and actuarial applications of machine learning. He is co-author of the book 
  <em>Hamiltonian Monte Carlo Methods in Machine Learning</em> (Elsevier, 2023) 
  and has received recognition including the Mail & Guardian 200 Young South 
  Africans award and the International Association of Consulting Actuaries Best 
  Paper Award (2020). His work connects methodological innovation in computational 
  statistics with pressing challenges in climate risk, public health, and insurance.
</p>
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- ──── Dr. Raeesa Manjoo-Docrat ──── -->
    <div class="row g-4 mb-4">
      <div class="col-lg-4">
        <div class="team-card teal">
          <div class="team-card-top"></div>
          <div style="padding: 2rem 1.5rem;">
           <img src="images/team/Raeesa_D.jpg" alt="Dr. Raeesa Manjoo-Docrat" class="team-avatar" style="object-fit: cover;">
            <div class="team-name">Dr. Raeesa Manjoo-Docrat</div>
            <div class="team-role">Senior Researcher & Lecturer</div>
            <div class="team-affiliation">University of the Witwatersrand</div>
            <div>
             <span class="team-badge">Spatial Statistics</span>
<span class="team-badge">Infectious Disease Modelling</span>
<span class="team-badge">Epidemiology</span>
<span class="team-badge">Stochastic Processes</span>
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>
      </div>
      <div class="col-lg-8">
        <div class="team-bio" style="border: 1px solid #eee; border-radius: 12px; padding: 2rem; height: 100%; display: flex; flex-direction: column; justify-content: center;">
          <p style="color: #444; font-size: 0.95rem; line-height: 1.8; margin-bottom: 1rem;">
  Dr. Raeesa Manjoo-Docrat has been a lecturer at WITS since 2018, following 
  an impressive academic trajectory marked by multiple accolades. She holds 
  a BSc in Mathematics, a Postgraduate Diploma in Actuarial Science and 
  Mathematical Statistics, and dual Honours degrees in Mathematics and 
  Statistics,the latter earning her the Liberty Medal for best honours student. 
  She completed her MSc in Mathematical Statistics with distinction in 2018, 
  focusing on stochastic processes and generating functions in branching 
  processes. Her PhD, completed in 2022, centred on spatio-stochastic 
  modelling of infectious diseases.
</p>
<p style="color: #444; font-size: 0.95rem; line-height: 1.8; margin-bottom: 1rem;">
  Her research sits at the intersection of infectious disease dynamics and 
  spatial statistics, with particular attention to heterogeneous populations. 
  She has collaborated with researchers at the CSIR, University of Pretoria, 
  and the South African Medical Research Council on COVID-19 modelling using 
  both spatial and national frameworks. Her 2025 open-access publication in 
  <em>Heliyon</em> on spatial modelling of the South African pandemic response 
  exemplifies her approach: integrating mathematical rigour with pressing 
  public health questions. She also spent a year as a postdoctoral fellow 
  at the Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health from 2023 to 2024, 
  expanding her work on applied epidemiological modelling.
</p>
<p style="color: #444; font-size: 0.95rem; line-height: 1.8;">
  Beyond research, she teaches applied statistics, spatial statistics, and 
  survival analysis at undergraduate and postgraduate levels. She is an active 
  member of R-Ladies, where she has led sessions on RShiny development and SIR 
  model implementation, and serves as a mentor with the STEM MentHer programme. 
  She has also co-organised and judged DataFest, a national student data 
  challenge. Outside academia, she runs long-distance and bakes,pursuits she 
  describes as the right balance of endurance and creativity.
</p>
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- ──── Dr. Justine Nasejje ──── -->
    <div class="row g-4 mb-4">
      <div class="col-lg-4">
        <div class="team-card gold">
          <div class="team-card-top"></div>
          <div style="padding: 2rem 1.5rem;">
            <img src="images/team/Justine_N.png" alt="Dr. Justine Nasejje" class="team-avatar" style="object-fit: cover;">
            <div class="team-name">Dr. Justine Nasejje</div>
            <div class="team-role">Postdoctoral Researcher</div>
            <div class="team-affiliation">University of the Witwatersrand</div>
            <div>
              <span class="team-badge">Survival Analysis</span>
<span class="team-badge">Machine Learning</span>
<span class="team-badge">Neural Networks</span>
<span class="team-badge">Biostatistics</span>
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>
      </div>
      <div class="col-lg-8">
        <div class="team-bio" style="border: 1px solid #eee; border-radius: 12px; padding: 2rem; height: 100%; display: flex; flex-direction: column; justify-content: center;">
            <p style="color: #444; font-size: 0.95rem; line-height: 1.8; margin-bottom: 1rem;">
  Dr. Justine Nasejje serves as Senior Lecturer at WITS, where she specialises 
  in survival analysis and machine learning methods. Her academic journey began 
  in Uganda, where she completed her BSc in Education at Makerere University 
  in 2007, supported by a government bursary. She chose to major in Mathematics 
  rather than the more popular Economics track, a decision that raised eyebrows 
  among her peers. She subsequently received a scholarship to the African 
  Institute for Mathematical Sciences (AIMS) in 2012, followed by an MSc and 
  PhD at the University of KwaZulu-Natal funded by DAAD. Her 2018 doctoral 
  thesis on random survival forests earned the Kambule Doctoral Award, and 
  her work has since appeared in <em>BMC Medical Research Methodology</em> and 
  <em>BMC Public Health</em>.
</p>
<p style="color: #444; font-size: 0.95rem; line-height: 1.8; margin-bottom: 1rem;">
  Her current research focuses on developing neural network methods for survival 
  analysis, extending her earlier applications of machine learning to 
  under-five mortality studies in Uganda. She has been a member of the 
  International Biometric Society since 2015 and has held research positions 
  at institutions including the Leibniz-Institut für Präventionsforschung und 
  Epidemiologie in Bremen, Germany. Her contributions have been recognised 
  through awards from the London Mathematical Society (2023) and The World 
  Academy of Sciences (2019).
</p>
<p style="color: #444; font-size: 0.95rem; line-height: 1.8;">
  Alongside her academic work, she co-founded Tendeka, an initiative providing 
  mentorship and financial support to girls from underprivileged backgrounds. 
  Her own experiences navigating STEM as a young woman inform her commitment 
  to addressing the structural barriers that exclude women from the field.
</p>

        </div>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- ──── Dr. Raeesa Ganey ──── -->
    <div class="row g-4 mb-4">
      <div class="col-lg-4">
        <div class="team-card">
          <div class="team-card-top" style="background: linear-gradient(90deg, #003d82, #1a6b5a);"></div>
          <div style="padding: 2rem 1.5rem;">
            <img src="images/team/Raeesa_G.jpg" alt="Dr. Raeesa Ganey" class="team-avatar" style="object-fit: cover;">
            <div class="team-name">Dr. Raeesa Ganey</div>
            <div class="team-role">Lecturer & Researcher</div>
            <div class="team-affiliation">University of the Witwatersrand</div>
            <div>
              <span class="team-badge">Multivariate Analysis</span>
<span class="team-badge">Data Visualisation</span>
<span class="team-badge">Biplots</span>
<span class="team-badge">Statistical Education</span>
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>
      </div>
      <div class="col-lg-8">
        <div class="team-bio" style="border: 1px solid #eee; border-radius: 12px; padding: 2rem; height: 100%; display: flex; flex-direction: column; justify-content: center;">
            <p style="color: #444; font-size: 0.95rem; line-height: 1.8; margin-bottom: 1rem;">
  Dr. Raeesa Ganey has been part of the WITS community since 2015, when she joined 
  the School of Statistics and Actuarial Science as a lecturer. Her academic path 
  began at North-West University in Potchefstroom, where she completed her BSc in 
  Actuarial Science back in 2011. She then headed south to the University of Cape 
  Town, spending the next several years immersed in postgraduate studies,wrapping up 
  her Honours in 2012, Master's in 2014, and eventually her PhD in 2019.
</p>
<p style="color: #444; font-size: 0.95rem; line-height: 1.8;">
  These days she teaches second-year Mathematical Statistics and third-year 
  Multivariate Data Analysis, a course she actually introduced at the honours 
  level after completing her doctorate. Her research sits at the intersection of 
  multivariate analysis and visualisation, she's particularly known for her work on 
  biplots, where she's developed new ways to capture non-linear patterns in complex 
  data. Beyond WITS, she collaborates with Stellenbosch University's visualisation 
  group and stays active in the South African Statistical Association.
</p>
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- ──── Odey Mofokeng ──── -->
    <div class="row g-4 mb-4">
      <div class="col-lg-4">
        <div class="team-card">
          <div class="team-card-top" style="background: linear-gradient(90deg, #1a6b5a, #2a8b72);"></div>
          <div style="padding: 2rem 1.5rem;">
            <img src="images/team/Odey_M.jpg" alt="Odey Mofokeng" class="team-avatar" style="object-fit: cover;">
            <div class="team-name">Odey Mofokeng</div>
            <div class="team-role">Masters Student in Statistics</div>
            <div class="team-affiliation">University of the Witwatersrand</div>
            <div>
              <span class="team-badge">Bayesian Statistics</span>
<span class="team-badge">Spatial Modelling</span>
<span class="team-badge">Climate Risk</span>
<span class="team-badge">Copula Methods</span>
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>
      </div>
      <div class="col-lg-8">
        <div class="team-bio" style="border: 1px solid #eee; border-radius: 12px; padding: 2rem; height: 100%; display: flex; flex-direction: column; justify-content: center;">
<p style="color: #444; font-size: 0.95rem; line-height: 1.8; margin-bottom: 1rem;">
  Odey Mofokeng is an MSc student in Mathematical Statistics at WITS, where he 
  completed his honours degree with distinction in 2025. His honours research on 
  wastewater surveillance using regression Kriging earned the Peter Fridjhon Gold 
  Medal for Best Honours Research Project, along with the Liberty Sandton City 
  Voucher for Best Honours Student and First Prize at the 2025 SASA Annual 
  Conference.
</p>
<p style="color: #444; font-size: 0.95rem; line-height: 1.8;">
  He holds an undergraduate degree in Actuarial Science from WITS and is currently 
  developing a Bayesian spatial copula framework for climate risk assessment in 
  KwaZulu-Natal as his MSc thesis.
</p>
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>

    <!-- ──── Aphiwe Ngcobo ──── -->
    <div class="row g-4">
      <div class="col-lg-4">
        <div class="team-card">
          <div class="team-card-top" style="background: linear-gradient(90deg, #001a38, #002B5C);"></div>
          <div style="padding: 2rem 1.5rem;">
           <img src="images/team/Aphiwe_N.jpeg" alt="Aphiwe Ngcobo" class="team-avatar" style="object-fit: cover;">
            <div class="team-name">Aphiwe Ngcobo</div>
            <div class="team-role">Honours Student in Mathematical Statistics</div>
            <div class="team-affiliation">University of the Witwatersrand</div>
            <div>
              <span class="team-badge">Climate Risk</span>
              <span class="team-badge">Health Statistics</span>
              <span class="team-badge">Actuarial Science</span>
              <span class="team-badge">Environmental Data</span>
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>
      </div>
      <div class="col-lg-8">
        <div class="team-bio" style="border: 1px solid #eee; border-radius: 12px; padding: 2rem; height: 100%; display: flex; flex-direction: column; justify-content: center;">
          <p style="color: #444; font-size: 0.95rem; line-height: 1.8;">
           An Honours student in Mathematical Statistics who completed his undergraduate 
  degree in Actuarial Science at WITS in 2025. His honours 
  research focuses on climate exposure and health risk in Gauteng, examining how 
  extreme heat, drought, and heavy rainfall affect vulnerable populations in the region.
          </p>
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>
```
